1025	\subsection{Closure-order summary}
1026	\label{subsec:fga_pic_closure_order_summary}
1027	
1028	The seven sorries partition into three ranks:
1029	\begin{itemize}
1030	  \item \textbf{Rank 1} (independent, closeable from existing
1031	    Mathlib alone): none. Every sorry has either a sibling-chapter
1032	    dependency, a Mathlib gap, or a Route~C substrate dependency.
1033	  \item \textbf{Rank 2} (depends on sibling chapters or a
1034	    non-Riemann--Roch Mathlib gap; closeable in iter-199+ under the
1035	    carrier-soundness probe): Sorries~1, 2, 3, and~4.
1036	    \begin{itemize}
1037	      \item Sorries~1 and~2 are gated on the A.1.c
1038	        (\cref{chap:Picard_RelPicFunctor}) and A.2.b
1039	        (\cref{chap:Picard_QuotScheme}) sibling chapters
1040	        committing to a specific \texttt{PicSharp} and
1041	        \texttt{divFunctor} pin.
1042	      \item Sorry~3 (Abel map) is gated on Sorries~1 and~2.
1043	      \item Sorry~4 (smooth-proper quotient) is the substantive
1044	        structural lemma; closeable independently of the other six
1045	        by formalising the EGA IV~8.11.5 step and the Altman--Kleiman
1046	        descent.
1047	    \end{itemize}
1048	  \item \textbf{Rank 3} (requires the Route~C substrate; blocked
1049	    until the Riemann--Roch arc lands): Sorries~5, 6, and~7.
1050	    \begin{itemize}
1051	      \item Sorry~5 (Picard scheme existence) is the load-bearing
1052	        Route~C-blocked carrier via the $m$-regularity / Serre
1053	        vanishing dependency.
1054	      \item Sorry~6 (representability witness) inherits the Route~C
1055	        dependency of Sorry~5.
1056	      \item Sorry~7 (group-scheme structure) is Route~C-independent
1057	        in itself but depends on Sorry~6.
1058	    \end{itemize}
1059	\end{itemize}
1060	
1061	\medskip
1062	
1063	\textbf{Recommended first attempt under the carrier-soundness
1064	probe.} Sorry~4 (the \texttt{instHasSmoothProperQuotient}
1065	\texttt{⟨sorry⟩}) is the highest-value rank-2 target: it is a
1066	structural lemma whose discharge does not depend on any sibling
1067	chapter committing first, and after the iter-199 carrier-soundness
1068	refactor it isolates the only mathematical obligation of
1069	\cref{lem:smooth_proper_quotient} into a single instance
1070	constructor. The EGA IV~8.11.5 sub-step is bounded, and the
1071	Altman--Kleiman descent has a known proof sketch in
1072	\cref{lem:smooth_proper_quotient} of this chapter.
1073	
1074	\textbf{Recommended second tier under the carrier-soundness probe.}
1075	Sorries~1, 2, 3 (the carrier instances for
1076	\texttt{PicSharp}, \texttt{divFunctor}, \texttt{abelMap}) are
1077	discharged in lockstep once the sibling chapters
1078	\cref{chap:Picard_RelPicFunctor} and \cref{chap:Picard_QuotScheme}
1079	commit to their pins. The dispatch order across the three is
1080	1~$\to$~2~$\to$~3 (the Abel map needs both presheaves to exist).
1081	
1082	\textbf{Held until Route~C re-engagement.} Sorries~5, 6, 7 form a
1083	single dependency chain (5~$\to$~6~$\to$~7) all gated on the
1084	Riemann--Roch arc. The Route~C re-engagement is the strategic
1085	prerequisite for any progress on these three carriers; until it
1086	lands, the rank-2 work suffices to keep the carrier-soundness probe
1087	structurally complete and to allow downstream Route~A consumers to
1088	proceed under typeclass quantification.
1089	
1090	\section{Out of scope}
1091	\label{sec:fga_pic_out_of_scope}
1092	
1093	This chapter packages \emph{only} the representable Picard scheme
1094	\(\Pic_{C/k}\) as an assembly of sibling-chapter inputs, plus the
1095	group-scheme refinement. The following downstream constructions live in
1096	separate sibling chapters and are explicitly out of scope here:
1097	\begin{itemize}
1098	  \item \textbf{Identity component \(\Pic^0_{C/k}\)} (A.3) --- the open and
1099	    closed subgroup scheme of \(\Pic_{C/k}\) containing the unit and
1100	    consisting of those classes lying in the connected component of
1101	    \(\Pic_{C/k}\). Its dimension, smoothness, and abelian-variety
1102	    structure are the content of the A.3 chapter
1103	    \texttt{Picard\_Pic0AbelianVariety.tex} (planned), gated on this
1104	    chapter.
1105	  \item \textbf{Quot scheme representability} --- consumed by
1106	    \cref{thm:fga_pic_representability} via \texttt{thm:quot\_representable}
1107	    (\cref{chap:Picard_QuotScheme}), not re-proved here.
1108	  \item \textbf{\'Etale sheafification of \(\Pic^\sharp\)} ---
1109	    \cref{chap:Picard_RelPicFunctor} (A.1.c) produces
1110	    \(\Pic^\sharp_{(C/k)\et}\) from the set-valued presheaf of
1111	    \cref{chap:Picard_LineBundlePullback}; this chapter consumes the
1112	    sheaf as a black box.

1114	    --- exists when \(\Pic_{C/k}\) represents \(\Pic_{C/k}\) (as opposed to
1115	    its \'etale sheafification) and \(C/k\) has a section; out of scope
1116	    here. The Albanese / Abel--Jacobi consumption in
1117	    \cref{chap:AbelJacobi} works with the \'etale-sheafified
1118	    \(\Pic^\sharp_{(C/k)\et}\) representation and does not require the
1119	    Poincar\'e sheaf.
1120	  \item \textbf{Mumford's example} (Kleiman~\S 4 eg:Mumford), counter-examples
1121	    to representability for non-integral geometric fibers --- not
1122	    relevant to a smooth proper geometrically integral curve.
1123	\end{itemize}

1	\chapter{{\v C}ech computation of higher direct images $R^i f_*$ (unconditional)}
2	\label{chap:Cohomology_CechHigherDirectImage}
3	% archon:covers AlgebraicJacobian/Cohomology/CechHigherDirectImage.lean
4	% SOURCE: [Stacks Project], Cohomology of Schemes, \S{\v C}ech cohomology of
5	% quasi-coherent sheaves and \S Quasi-coherence of higher direct images
6	% (Lemmas \ref{lemma-cech-cohomology-quasi-coherent-trivial},
7	% \ref{lemma-quasi-coherent-affine-cohomology-zero},
8	% \ref{lemma-cech-cohomology-quasi-coherent},
9	% \ref{lemma-quasi-coherence-higher-direct-images-application},
10	% \ref{lemma-flat-base-change-cohomology}) (read from
11	% references/stacks-coherent.tex).
12	
13	\section{Setup and motivation}
14	
15	This chapter constructs the higher derived direct images \(R^i f_*\mathcal{F}\) for
16	\(i \geq 1\) \emph{without appealing to injective resolutions in the category of
17	sheaves of modules}. The companion development of
18	Chapter~\ref{chap:Cohomology_HigherDirectImage} defines \(R^i f_*\) as a right
19	derived functor, which in a formalisation requires the ambient category of
20	\(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules to have enough injectives; that property is not currently
21	available for sheaves of modules over a sheaf of rings whose value category varies
22	over the site. The {\v C}ech approach developed here sidesteps the issue entirely:
23	it computes \(R^i f_*\mathcal{F}\) as the cohomology of an explicit complex built
24	from the pushforwards of \(\mathcal{F}\) over the finite intersections of an affine
25	open cover, and so produces an \emph{unconditional} construction of \(R^i f_*\) for
26	quasi-coherent \(\mathcal{F}\) and separated quasi-compact \(f\).
27	
28	Throughout, \(f : X \to S\) is a morphism of schemes that is quasi-compact and
29	separated (so that all finite intersections of an affine open cover of \(X\) are
30	again affine), and \(\mathcal{F}\) is a quasi-coherent \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-module. We
31	write \(\operatorname{QCoh}(X)\) for the category of quasi-coherent
32	\(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules. A base change of \(f\) along a morphism \(g : S' \to S\)
33	is recorded by the cartesian square obtained from the fibre product
34	\(X' = X \times_S S'\):
35	\[
36	  \begin{array}{ccc}
37	    X' & \xrightarrow{\;g'\;} & X \\[2pt]
38	    {\scriptstyle f'}\big\downarrow & & \big\downarrow{\scriptstyle f} \\[2pt]
39	    S' & \xrightarrow{\;g\;} & S
40	  \end{array}
41	\]
42	Here \(f' : X' \to S'\) is the base change of \(f\), \(g' : X' \to X\) is the first
43	projection, and \(\mathcal{F}' = (g')^*\mathcal{F}\) is the pullback of
44	\(\mathcal{F}\) to \(X'\).
45	
46	\section{The {\v C}ech nerve and {\v C}ech complex of an affine cover}
47	
48	\begin{definition}
49	[{\v C}ech nerve of an affine open cover]
50	  \label{def:cech_nerve}
51	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.CechNerve}
52	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], Cohomology of Schemes, \S{\v C}ech cohomology of
53	  % quasi-coherent sheaves, opening paragraph and Lemma
54	  % \ref{lemma-cech-cohomology-quasi-coherent-trivial}
55	  % (read from references/stacks-coherent.tex, L36--67).
56	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "Let $X$ be a scheme.
57	  % Let $U \subset X$ be an affine open.
58	  % Recall that a {\it standard open covering} of $U$ is a covering
59	  % of the form $\mathcal{U} : U = \bigcup_{i = 1}^n D(f_i)$
60	  % where $f_1, \ldots, f_n \in \Gamma(U, \mathcal{O}_X)$ generate
61	  % the unit ideal, see
62	  % Schemes, Definition \ref{schemes-definition-standard-covering}."
63	  \textit{Source: Stacks Project, Cohomology of Schemes, \S{\v C}ech cohomology of
64	  quasi-coherent sheaves.}
65	  Let \(X\) be a scheme and let
66	  \(\mathcal{U} : X = \bigcup_{i \in I} U_i\) be a finite open cover of \(X\) by
67	  affine opens, with index set \(I\). For a finite nonempty tuple
68	  \(i_0, \ldots, i_p \in I\) write
69	  \(U_{i_0 \ldots i_p} = U_{i_0} \cap \cdots \cap U_{i_p}\) for the corresponding
70	  intersection. The \emph{{\v C}ech nerve} of \(\mathcal{U}\) is the augmented
71	  simplicial object in \(\operatorname{QCoh}(X)\) whose object in simplicial degree
72	  \(p\) is the direct image, along the open immersions
73	  \(j_{i_0 \ldots i_p} : U_{i_0 \ldots i_p} \hookrightarrow X\), of the restriction
74	  of \(\mathcal{O}_X\) (or of any chosen quasi-coherent sheaf) to the
75	  \((p+1)\)-fold intersections,
76	  \[
77	    [p] \;\longmapsto\;
78	      \prod_{(i_0, \ldots, i_p) \in I^{p+1}}
79	        (j_{i_0 \ldots i_p})_* \,\bigl(\,\cdot\,|_{U_{i_0 \ldots i_p}}\bigr),
80	  \]
81	  with faces given by the restriction maps that omit one index and degeneracies by
82	  the maps that repeat one index; the augmentation in degree \(-1\) is the object
83	  on all of \(X\). When \(X\) is separated each intersection
84	  \(U_{i_0 \ldots i_p}\) is itself affine. The local affine model is the
85	  \emph{standard open covering} \(U = \bigcup_{i=1}^n D(f_i)\) of an affine open
86	  \(U = \operatorname{Spec}(A)\) by basic opens, where \(f_1, \ldots, f_n \in A\)
87	  generate the unit ideal; on such a cover the intersections are again basic opens
88	  \(D(f_{i_0} \cdots f_{i_p})\).
89	\end{definition}
90	
91	\begin{definition}
92	[{\v C}ech complex of a quasi-coherent sheaf]
93	  \label{def:cech_complex}
94	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.CechComplex}
95	  \uses{def:cech_nerve}
96	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], Cohomology of Schemes, Lemma
97	  % \ref{lemma-cech-cohomology-quasi-coherent-trivial} (proof)
98	  % (read from references/stacks-coherent.tex, L59--77).
99	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "Clearly the {\v C}ech complex
100	  % $\check{\mathcal{C}}^\bullet(\mathcal{U}, \mathcal{F})$
101	  % is identified with the complex
102	  % $$
103	  % \prod\nolimits_{i_0} M_{f_{i_0}} \to
104	  % \prod\nolimits_{i_0i_1} M_{f_{i_0}f_{i_1}} \to
105	  % \prod\nolimits_{i_0i_1i_2} M_{f_{i_0}f_{i_1}f_{i_2}} \to
106	  % \ldots
107	  % $$
108	  % We are asked to show that the extended complex
109	  % \begin{equation}
110	  % \label{equation-extended}
111	  % 0 \to
112	  % M \to
113	  % \prod\nolimits_{i_0} M_{f_{i_0}} \to
114	  % \prod\nolimits_{i_0i_1} M_{f_{i_0}f_{i_1}} \to
115	  % \prod\nolimits_{i_0i_1i_2} M_{f_{i_0}f_{i_1}f_{i_2}} \to
116	  % \ldots
117	  % \end{equation}
118	  % (whose truncation we have studied in
119	  % Algebra, Lemma \ref{algebra-lemma-cover-module}) is exact."
120	  \textit{Source: Stacks Project, Cohomology of Schemes, Lemma
121	  \ref{lemma-cech-cohomology-quasi-coherent-trivial}.}
122	  Let \(\mathcal{F}\) be a quasi-coherent \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-module and let
123	  \(\mathcal{U} : X = \bigcup_{i \in I} U_i\) be a finite affine open cover with
124	  all intersections \(U_{i_0 \ldots i_p}\) affine (e.g.\ \(X\) separated). The
125	  \emph{{\v C}ech complex} \(\check{\mathcal{C}}^\bullet(\mathcal{U}, \mathcal{F})\)
126	  is the cochain complex obtained by applying the global-sections functor to the
127	  {\v C}ech nerve of Definition~\ref{def:cech_nerve} evaluated at \(\mathcal{F}\);
128	  in degree \(p\) it is
129	  \[
130	    \check{\mathcal{C}}^p(\mathcal{U}, \mathcal{F})
131	      = \prod_{(i_0, \ldots, i_p) \in I^{p+1}}
132	        \mathcal{F}\bigl(U_{i_0 \ldots i_p}\bigr),
133	  \]
134	  with differential \(d : \check{\mathcal{C}}^p \to \check{\mathcal{C}}^{p+1}\) the
135	  alternating sum \((d s)_{i_0 \ldots i_{p+1}} = \sum_{j=0}^{p+1} (-1)^j\,
136	  s_{i_0 \ldots \widehat{i_j} \ldots i_{p+1}}|_{U_{i_0 \ldots i_{p+1}}}\) of the
137	  restriction maps. Over an affine \(U = \operatorname{Spec}(A)\) with
138	  \(\mathcal{F}|_U = \widetilde{M}\) and a standard cover by the
139	  \(D(f_i)\), this is the complex
140	  \(\prod_{i_0} M_{f_{i_0}} \to \prod_{i_0 i_1} M_{f_{i_0} f_{i_1}} \to \cdots\) of
141	  localisations. The \emph{relative {\v C}ech complex} \(\check{\mathcal{C}}^\bullet
142	  (\mathfrak{U}, \mathcal{F})\) for \(f : X \to S\) is the analogous complex in
143	  \(\operatorname{QCoh}(S)\) obtained by applying \(f_*\) over each finite
144	  intersection instead of global sections; its terms are
145	  \(\prod_{i_0 \ldots i_p} (f|_{U_{i_0 \ldots i_p}})_*
146	  \bigl(\mathcal{F}|_{U_{i_0 \ldots i_p}}\bigr)\), and it is a complex in
147	  \(\operatorname{QCoh}(S)\) because each \(U_{i_0 \ldots i_p}\) is affine and the
148	  pushforward of a quasi-coherent sheaf along a quasi-compact quasi-separated
149	  morphism is quasi-coherent.
150	\end{definition}
151	
152	\section{Affine acyclicity of the {\v C}ech complex}
153	
154	\begin{lemma}
155	[{\v C}ech acyclicity on affines]
156	  \label{lem:cech_acyclic_affine}
157	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.CechAcyclic.affine}
158	  \uses{def:cech_complex}
159	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], Cohomology of Schemes, Tag 02KG (affine vanishing);
160	  % Lemma \ref{lemma-cech-cohomology-quasi-coherent-trivial} (standard-cover
161	  % {\v C}ech vanishing) and Lemma
162	  % \ref{lemma-quasi-coherent-affine-cohomology-zero} (Serre vanishing on affines)
163	  % (read from references/stacks-coherent.tex, L44--52 and L145--155).
164	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "Let $X$ be a scheme.
165	  % Let $\mathcal{F}$ be a quasi-coherent $\mathcal{O}_X$-module.
166	  % Let $\mathcal{U} : U = \bigcup_{i = 1}^n D(f_i)$ be a standard
167	  % open covering of an affine open of $X$.
168	  % Then $\check{H}^p(\mathcal{U}, \mathcal{F}) = 0$ for
169	  % all $p > 0$."
170	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "Serre vanishing: Higher cohomology vanishes on affine schemes
171	  % for quasi-coherent modules.
172	  % Let $X$ be a scheme.
173	  % Let $\mathcal{F}$ be a quasi-coherent $\mathcal{O}_X$-module.
174	  % For any affine open $U \subset X$ we have
175	  % $H^p(U, \mathcal{F}) = 0$ for all $p > 0$."
176	  \textit{Source: Stacks Project, Cohomology of Schemes, Lemmas
177	  \ref{lemma-cech-cohomology-quasi-coherent-trivial} and
178	  \ref{lemma-quasi-coherent-affine-cohomology-zero} (Serre vanishing on affines;
179	  Tag 02KG).}
180	  Let \(U = \operatorname{Spec}(A)\) be an affine scheme, \(\mathcal{F}\) a
181	  quasi-coherent \(\mathcal{O}_U\)-module, and
182	  \(\mathcal{U} : U = \bigcup_{i=1}^n D(f_i)\) a standard open cover (so the
183	  \(f_i \in A\) generate the unit ideal). Then the {\v C}ech complex
184	  \(\check{\mathcal{C}}^\bullet(\mathcal{U}, \mathcal{F})\) has vanishing cohomology
185	  in all positive degrees:
186	  \[
187	    \check{H}^p(\mathcal{U}, \mathcal{F}) = 0 \qquad \text{for all } p > 0,
188	  \]
189	  and consequently \(H^p(U, \mathcal{F}) = 0\) for all \(p > 0\).
190	\end{lemma}
191	
192	% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: "Write $U = \Spec(A)$ for some ring $A$.
193	% In other words, $f_1, \ldots, f_n$ are elements of $A$
194	% which generate the unit ideal of $A$.
195	% Write $\mathcal{F}|_U = \widetilde{M}$ for some $A$-module $M$.
196	% ...
197	% It suffices to show that (\ref{equation-extended})
198	% is exact after localizing at a prime $\mathfrak p$, see
199	% Algebra, Lemma \ref{algebra-lemma-characterize-zero-local}.
200	% In fact we will show that the extended complex localized
201	% at $\mathfrak p$ is homotopic to zero.
202	% There exists an index $i$ such that $f_i \not \in \mathfrak p$.
203	% Choose and fix such an element $i_{\text{fix}}$. ...
204	% Let us define a homotopy
205	% $$
206	% h :
207	% \prod\nolimits_{i_0 \ldots i_{p + 1}}
208	% M_{f_{i_0} \ldots f_{i_{p + 1}}, \mathfrak p}
209	% \longrightarrow
210	% \prod\nolimits_{i_0 \ldots i_p}
211	% M_{f_{i_0} \ldots f_{i_p}, \mathfrak p}
212	% $$
213	% by the rule
214	% $$
215	% h(s)_{i_0 \ldots i_p} = s_{i_{\text{fix}} i_0 \ldots i_p}
216	% $$
217	% ... We compute ... $(dh + hd)(s)_{i_0 \ldots i_p} = s_{i_0 \ldots i_p}$ ...
218	% This proves the identity map is homotopic to zero as desired."
219	% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: "We are going to apply
220	% Cohomology, Lemma \ref{cohomology-lemma-cech-vanish-basis}.
221	% As our basis $\mathcal{B}$ for the topology of $X$ we are going to use
222	% the affine opens of $X$. ... Note that the intersection of standard opens
223	% in an affine is another standard open. Hence property (1) holds. ...
224	% Finally, condition (3) of the lemma follows from
225	% Lemma \ref{lemma-cech-cohomology-quasi-coherent-trivial}."
226	\begin{proof}
227	  \uses{def:cech_complex}
228	  Write \(U = \operatorname{Spec}(A)\) and \(\mathcal{F}|_U = \widetilde{M}\) for an
229	  \(A\)-module \(M\). By Definition~\ref{def:cech_complex} the {\v C}ech complex of
230	  the standard cover is the complex of localisations
231	  \[
232	    \prod_{i_0} M_{f_{i_0}} \;\to\;
233	    \prod_{i_0 i_1} M_{f_{i_0} f_{i_1}} \;\to\;
234	    \prod_{i_0 i_1 i_2} M_{f_{i_0} f_{i_1} f_{i_2}} \;\to\; \cdots,
235	  \]
236	  and the claim \(\check{H}^p = 0\) for \(p > 0\) is equivalent to exactness of the
237	  extended complex \(0 \to M \to \prod_{i_0} M_{f_{i_0}} \to \cdots\). Exactness may
238	  be checked after localising at an arbitrary prime \(\mathfrak{p} \subset A\).
239	  Choose an index \(i_{\mathrm{fix}}\) with
240	  \(f_{i_{\mathrm{fix}}} \notin \mathfrak{p}\); then
241	  \(M_{f_{i_{\mathrm{fix}}}, \mathfrak{p}} = M_{\mathfrak{p}}\), and the
242	  prescription \(h(s)_{i_0 \ldots i_p} = s_{i_{\mathrm{fix}} i_0 \ldots i_p}\)
243	  defines a contracting homotopy of the localised extended complex: a direct
244	  computation gives \((dh + hd)(s) = s\), so the identity is homotopic to zero and
245	  the localised complex is exact. As exactness holds after every localisation, the
246	  extended complex is exact and \(\check{H}^p(\mathcal{U}, \mathcal{F}) = 0\) for
247	  \(p > 0\).
248	
249	  For the sheaf-cohomology statement, take the affine opens of \(U\) as a basis of
250	  the topology and the standard open covers as the admissible covers. Intersections
251	  of standard opens are again standard opens, standard covers are cofinal among
252	  covers of a basic open, and the {\v C}ech vanishing just proved supplies the
253	  acyclicity hypothesis; the {\v C}ech-to-cohomology comparison on a basis then
254	  yields \(H^p(U, \mathcal{F}) = 0\) for all \(p > 0\). \emph{(This is Serre
255	  vanishing for quasi-coherent sheaves on affine schemes.)}
256	\end{proof}
257	
258	\section{The {\v C}ech complex computes $R^i f_*$}
259	
260	\begin{lemma}
261	[{\v C}ech complex computes the higher direct images]
262	  \label{lem:cech_computes_cohomology}
263	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.cech_computes_higherDirectImage}
264	  \uses{lem:cech_acyclic_affine, def:cech_complex}
265	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], Cohomology of Schemes, Tag 02KE; Lemma
266	  % \ref{lemma-cech-cohomology-quasi-coherent} ({\v C}ech computes cohomology when
267	  % all intersections are affine) and Lemma
268	  % \ref{lemma-quasi-coherence-higher-direct-images-application}
269	  % ($H^q(X,\mathcal{F}) = H^0(S, R^q f_*\mathcal{F})$ for affine $S$)
270	  % (read from references/stacks-coherent.tex, L245--256 and L843--854).
271	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "Let $X$ be a scheme.
272	  % Let $\mathcal{U} : X = \bigcup_{i \in I} U_i$ be an open covering such that
273	  % $U_{i_0 \ldots i_p}$ is affine open for all $p \ge 0$ and all
274	  % $i_0, \ldots, i_p \in I$.
275	  % In this case for any quasi-coherent sheaf $\mathcal{F}$ we have
276	  % $$
277	  % \check{H}^p(\mathcal{U}, \mathcal{F}) = H^p(X, \mathcal{F})
278	  % $$
279	  % as $\Gamma(X, \mathcal{O}_X)$-modules for all $p$."
280	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "Let $f : X \to S$ be a morphism of schemes.
281	  % Assume that $f$ is quasi-separated and quasi-compact.
282	  % Assume $S$ is affine.
283	  % For any quasi-coherent $\mathcal{O}_X$-module $\mathcal{F}$
284	  % we have
285	  % $$
286	  % H^q(X, \mathcal{F}) = H^0(S, R^qf_*\mathcal{F})
287	  % $$
288	  % for all $q \in \mathbf{Z}$."
289	  \textit{Source: Stacks Project, Cohomology of Schemes, Lemmas
290	  \ref{lemma-cech-cohomology-quasi-coherent} and
291	  \ref{lemma-quasi-coherence-higher-direct-images-application} (Tag 02KE).}
292	  Let \(f : X \to S\) be a separated quasi-compact morphism of schemes, let
293	  \(\mathcal{F}\) be a quasi-coherent \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-module, and let
294	  \(\mathfrak{U} : X = \bigcup_{i \in I} U_i\) be a finite affine open cover of
295	  \(X\) (so, by separatedness, every intersection \(U_{i_0 \ldots i_p}\) is
296	  affine). Then the cohomology sheaves of the relative {\v C}ech complex
297	  \(\check{\mathcal{C}}^\bullet(\mathfrak{U}, \mathcal{F})\) of
298	  Definition~\ref{def:cech_complex} compute the higher direct images: for every
299	  \(i \geq 0\) there is a canonical isomorphism of \(\mathcal{O}_S\)-modules
300	  \[
301	    H^i\bigl(\check{\mathcal{C}}^\bullet(\mathfrak{U}, \mathcal{F})\bigr)
302	      \;\cong\; R^i f_*\mathcal{F}.
303	  \]
304	  In particular, over an affine base \(S = \operatorname{Spec}(A)\), taking global
305	  sections gives \(H^i(X, \mathcal{F}) = \check{H}^i(\mathfrak{U}, \mathcal{F}) =
306	  H^0(S, R^i f_*\mathcal{F})\) as \(A\)-modules for all \(i\).
307	\end{lemma}
308	
309	% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: "In view of
310	% Lemma \ref{lemma-quasi-coherent-affine-cohomology-zero}
311	% this is a special case of
312	% Cohomology, Lemma
313	% \ref{cohomology-lemma-cech-spectral-sequence-application}."
314	% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: "Consider the Leray spectral sequence
315	% $E_2^{p, q} = H^p(S, R^qf_*\mathcal{F})$
316	% converging to $H^{p + q}(X, \mathcal{F})$, see
317	% Cohomology, Lemma \ref{cohomology-lemma-Leray}.
318	% By Lemma \ref{lemma-quasi-coherence-higher-direct-images}
319	% we see that the sheaves $R^qf_*\mathcal{F}$ are quasi-coherent.
320	% By Lemma \ref{lemma-quasi-coherent-affine-cohomology-zero}
321	% we see that $E_2^{p, q} = 0$ when $p > 0$.
322	% Hence the spectral sequence degenerates at $E_2$ and we win."
323	\begin{proof}
324	  \uses{lem:cech_acyclic_affine, def:cech_complex}
325	  The question is local on \(S\), and the formation of both sides commutes with
326	  restriction to opens of \(S\), so we may assume \(S = \operatorname{Spec}(A)\) is
327	  affine. Because \(f\) is separated and quasi-compact, each finite intersection
328	  \(U_{i_0 \ldots i_p}\) is affine, and \(f\) restricted to it is an affine
329	  morphism; by the affine acyclicity of Lemma~\ref{lem:cech_acyclic_affine} the
330	  higher direct images of \(\mathcal{F}\) over each such intersection vanish, so the
331	  terms of the relative {\v C}ech complex carry no hidden higher cohomology.
332	
333	  Consider the {\v C}ech-to-cohomology spectral sequence of the cover
334	  \(\mathfrak{U}\), with \(E_2\)-page
335	  \(E_2^{p,q} = \check{H}^p(\mathfrak{U}, \underline{H}^q(\mathcal{F}))\)
336	  converging to \(H^{p+q}(X, \mathcal{F})\). Since all intersections are affine,
337	  Lemma~\ref{lem:cech_acyclic_affine} forces the presheaf cohomology
338	  \(\underline{H}^q(\mathcal{F})\) to vanish on the intersections for \(q > 0\), so
339	  the spectral sequence collapses to its row \(q = 0\): the {\v C}ech cohomology of
340	  \(\mathcal{F}\) for the cover \(\mathfrak{U}\) computes the sheaf cohomology,
341	  \(\check{H}^p(\mathfrak{U}, \mathcal{F}) = H^p(X, \mathcal{F})\) for all \(p\),
342	  and the same identification holds relatively, i.e.\ the cohomology sheaves of
343	  \(\check{\mathcal{C}}^\bullet(\mathfrak{U}, \mathcal{F})\) compute the cohomology
344	  sheaves of \(Rf_*\mathcal{F}\).
345	
346	  It remains to identify these cohomology sheaves with \(R^i f_*\mathcal{F}\). Over
347	  the affine base \(S = \operatorname{Spec}(A)\), the Leray spectral sequence
348	  \(E_2^{p,q} = H^p(S, R^q f_*\mathcal{F}) \Rightarrow H^{p+q}(X, \mathcal{F})\)
349	  has \(R^q f_*\mathcal{F}\) quasi-coherent, whence \(H^p(S, R^q f_*\mathcal{F}) = 0\)
350	  for \(p > 0\) by Serre vanishing (Lemma~\ref{lem:cech_acyclic_affine}). The Leray
351	  sequence therefore degenerates at \(E_2\), giving
352	  \(H^i(X, \mathcal{F}) = H^0(S, R^i f_*\mathcal{F})\). Combining with the previous
353	  paragraph, \(H^i\bigl(\check{\mathcal{C}}^\bullet(\mathfrak{U}, \mathcal{F})\bigr)
354	  \cong R^i f_*\mathcal{F}\) as \(\mathcal{O}_S\)-modules for all \(i \geq 0\).
355	\end{proof}

357	\section{The unconditional higher direct image}
358	
359	\begin{definition}
360	[Unconditional higher direct image via {\v C}ech]
361	  \label{def:cech_higher_direct_image}
362	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.cechHigherDirectImage}
363	  \uses{lem:cech_computes_cohomology}
364	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], Cohomology of Schemes, Lemma
365	  % \ref{lemma-quasi-coherence-higher-direct-images-application}
366	  % (read from references/stacks-coherent.tex, L843--854); the unconditional
367	  % packaging via the {\v C}ech complex is an Archon-original construction grounded
368	  % in this lemma and Lemma \ref{lemma-cech-cohomology-quasi-coherent}.
369	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "Let $f : X \to S$ be a morphism of schemes.
370	  % Assume that $f$ is quasi-separated and quasi-compact.
371	  % Assume $S$ is affine.
372	  % For any quasi-coherent $\mathcal{O}_X$-module $\mathcal{F}$
373	  % we have
374	  % $$
375	  % H^q(X, \mathcal{F}) = H^0(S, R^qf_*\mathcal{F})
376	  % $$
377	  % for all $q \in \mathbf{Z}$."
378	  \textit{Source: Stacks Project, Cohomology of Schemes, Lemma
379	  \ref{lemma-quasi-coherence-higher-direct-images-application}; unconditional
380	  {\v C}ech packaging is Archon-original.}
381	  Let \(f : X \to S\) be a separated quasi-compact morphism and \(\mathcal{F}\) a
382	  quasi-coherent \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-module. Fix a finite affine open cover
383	  \(\mathfrak{U}\) of \(X\). The \emph{(unconditional) \(i\)-th higher direct
384	  image} is defined, for each \(i \geq 0\), as the \(i\)-th cohomology sheaf of the
385	  relative {\v C}ech complex,
386	  \[
387	    R^i f_*\mathcal{F} \;:=\;
388	      H^i\bigl(\check{\mathcal{C}}^\bullet(\mathfrak{U}, \mathcal{F})\bigr)
389	      \in \operatorname{QCoh}(S).
390	  \]
391	  This requires \emph{no} hypothesis that the category of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules
392	  have enough injectives: the right-hand side is the cohomology of an explicit
393	  complex of quasi-coherent sheaves. By
394	  Lemma~\ref{lem:cech_computes_cohomology} the result is canonically isomorphic to
395	  the derived-functor higher direct image wherever the latter is defined, and in
396	  particular is independent of the chosen affine cover \(\mathfrak{U}\) up to
397	  canonical isomorphism: two affine covers admit a common affine refinement, and
398	  the induced refinement maps on {\v C}ech complexes are quasi-isomorphisms because
399	  both compute the same \(R^i f_*\mathcal{F}\) by
400	  Lemma~\ref{lem:cech_computes_cohomology}. For \(i = 0\) one recovers the ordinary
401	  pushforward \(R^0 f_*\mathcal{F} = f_*\mathcal{F}\).
402	\end{definition}

404	\section{Flat base change for the {\v C}ech higher direct images}
405	
406	\begin{lemma}
407	[Flat base change of $R^i f_*$ via {\v C}ech]
408	  \label{lem:cech_flat_base_change}
409	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.cech_flatBaseChange}
410	  \uses{def:cech_higher_direct_image, lem:cech_computes_cohomology}
411	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], Cohomology of Schemes, Tag 02KH (Lemma
412	  % \ref{lemma-flat-base-change-cohomology}, "Flat base change")
413	  % (read from references/stacks-coherent.tex, L947--970).
414	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "Consider a cartesian diagram of schemes
415	  % $$
416	  % \xymatrix{
417	  % X' \ar[d]_{f'} \ar[r]_{g'} & X \ar[d]^f \\
418	  % S' \ar[r]^g & S
419	  % }
420	  % $$
421	  % Let $\mathcal{F}$ be a quasi-coherent $\mathcal{O}_X$-module
422	  % with pullback $\mathcal{F}' = (g')^*\mathcal{F}$.
423	  % Assume that $g$ is flat and that $f$ is quasi-compact and quasi-separated.
424	  % For any $i \geq 0$
425	  % \begin{enumerate}
426	  % \item the base change map of
427	  % Cohomology, Lemma \ref{cohomology-lemma-base-change-map-flat-case}
428	  % is an isomorphism
429	  % $$
430	  % g^*R^if_*\mathcal{F} \longrightarrow R^if'_*\mathcal{F}',
431	  % $$
432	  % \item if $S = \Spec(A)$ and $S' = \Spec(B)$, then
433	  % $H^i(X, \mathcal{F}) \otimes_A B = H^i(X', \mathcal{F}')$.
434	  % \end{enumerate}"
435	  \textit{Source: Stacks Project, Cohomology of Schemes, Lemma
436	  \ref{lemma-flat-base-change-cohomology} (Tag 02KH, ``Flat base change'').}
437	  Consider the cartesian square
438	  \[
439	    \begin{array}{ccc}
440	      X' & \xrightarrow{\;g'\;} & X \\[2pt]
441	      {\scriptstyle f'}\big\downarrow & & \big\downarrow{\scriptstyle f} \\[2pt]
442	      S' & \xrightarrow{\;g\;} & S
443	    \end{array}
444	  \]
445	  with \(f : X \to S\) separated and quasi-compact, \(\mathcal{F}\) a
446	  quasi-coherent \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-module, and
447	  \(\mathcal{F}' = (g')^*\mathcal{F}\). Assume \(g\) is flat. Then for every
448	  \(i \geq 0\) the canonical base-change map between the unconditional {\v C}ech
449	  higher direct images of Definition~\ref{def:cech_higher_direct_image} is an
450	  isomorphism
451	  \[
452	    g^*\bigl(R^i f_*\mathcal{F}\bigr) \;\xrightarrow{\;\sim\;}\;
453	      R^i f'_*\bigl((g')^*\mathcal{F}\bigr) = R^i f'_*\mathcal{F}'.
454	  \]
455	  Equivalently, when \(S = \operatorname{Spec}(A)\) and
456	  \(S' = \operatorname{Spec}(B)\) with \(A \to B\) flat, the comparison map of
457	  \(B\)-modules \(H^i(X, \mathcal{F}) \otimes_A B \to H^i(X', \mathcal{F}')\) is an
458	  isomorphism.
459	\end{lemma}
460	
461	% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: "Having said this, part (1) follows from part (2). Namely,
462	% part (1) is local on $S'$ and hence we may assume $S$
463	% and $S'$ are affine. ...
464	% In case $X$ is separated, choose an affine open covering
465	% $\mathcal{U} : X = U_1 \cup \ldots \cup U_t$ and recall that
466	% $$
467	% \check{H}^p(\mathcal{U}, \mathcal{F}) = H^p(X, \mathcal{F}),
468	% $$
469	% see
470	% Lemma \ref{lemma-cech-cohomology-quasi-coherent}.
471	% If $\mathcal{U}_B : X_B = (U_1)_B \cup \ldots \cup (U_t)_B$ we obtain
472	% by base change, then it is still the case that each $(U_i)_B$ is affine
473	% and that $X_B$ is separated. ...
474	% We have the following relation between the {\v C}ech complexes
475	% $$
476	% \check{\mathcal{C}}^\bullet(\mathcal{U}_B, \mathcal{F}_B) =
477	% \check{\mathcal{C}}^\bullet(\mathcal{U}, \mathcal{F}) \otimes_A B
478	% $$
479	% as follows from
480	% Lemma \ref{lemma-affine-base-change}.
481	% Since $A \to B$ is flat, the same thing remains true on taking cohomology."
482	\begin{proof}
483	  \uses{def:cech_higher_direct_image, lem:cech_computes_cohomology}
484	  The assertion that the base-change map is an isomorphism is local on \(S'\), so we
485	  may assume \(S = \operatorname{Spec}(A)\) and \(S' = \operatorname{Spec}(B)\) with
486	  \(A \to B\) flat. By Lemma~\ref{lem:cech_computes_cohomology} both
487	  \(R^i f_*\mathcal{F}\) and \(R^i f'_*\mathcal{F}'\) are quasi-coherent, associated
488	  to the \(A\)-module \(H^i(X, \mathcal{F})\) and the \(B\)-module
489	  \(H^i(X', \mathcal{F}')\) respectively; since pullback along \(g\) corresponds to
490	  \(- \otimes_A B\) on modules, it suffices to prove that the comparison map
491	  \(H^i(X, \mathcal{F}) \otimes_A B \to H^i(X', \mathcal{F}')\) is an isomorphism.
492	
493	  Choose a finite affine open cover \(\mathfrak{U} : X = U_1 \cup \dots \cup U_t\);
494	  by separatedness all intersections \(U_{i_0 \ldots i_p}\) are affine. Base
495	  changing the cover yields an affine open cover \(\mathfrak{U}_B\) of the
496	  again-separated \(X' = X_B\), with each \((U_{i_0 \ldots i_p})_B\) affine. By
497	  Lemma~\ref{lem:cech_computes_cohomology} the two {\v C}ech complexes compute the
498	  respective cohomologies. The affine base change for the \(i = 0\) direct image
499	  identifies each term of the base-changed {\v C}ech complex with the original term
500	  tensored over \(A\) with \(B\), giving an isomorphism of cochain complexes
501	  \[
502	    \check{\mathcal{C}}^\bullet(\mathfrak{U}_B, \mathcal{F}_B)
503	      \;\cong\;
504	      \check{\mathcal{C}}^\bullet(\mathfrak{U}, \mathcal{F}) \otimes_A B.
505	  \]
506	  Because \(A \to B\) is flat, \(- \otimes_A B\) is exact and commutes with the
507	  formation of cohomology of a complex; applying \(H^i\) for any \(i \geq 0\) yields
508	  \[
509	    H^i(X, \mathcal{F}) \otimes_A B \;\xrightarrow{\;\sim\;}\;
510	      H^i(X', \mathcal{F}').
511	  \]
512	  Translating back to sheaves on \(S' = \operatorname{Spec}(B)\) — using that the
513	  unconditional higher direct images are the quasi-coherent sheaves associated to
514	  these modules — gives the asserted isomorphism
515	  \(g^*(R^i f_*\mathcal{F}) \xrightarrow{\sim} R^i f'_*\mathcal{F}'\) for all
516	  \(i \geq 0\). The flatness of \(g\) (hence of \(g'\), by base change) is exactly
517	  what makes \(- \otimes_A B\) exact, and is therefore the precise hypothesis under
518	  which the {\v C}ech computation of Definition~\ref{def:cech_higher_direct_image}
519	  is compatible with base change.
520	\end{proof}

2	\label{chap:Picard_TensorObjSubstrate}

8	\section{Motivation: the abelian-group-valued relative Picard sheaf}
9	\label{sec:tensorobj_motivation}
10	
11	Fix a base field \(k\) and a smooth proper geometrically integral curve \(C\) over
12	\(k\). In Kleiman's framework (\cref{chap:Picard_RelPicFunctor},
13	\cref{def:rel_pic_sharp}), the relative Picard functor
14	\[
15	  \Pic_{C/k} \;\;\colon\;\; (\Sch/k)^{op} \;\longrightarrow\; \AddCommGrpCat,
16	  \qquad
17	  T \;\longmapsto\; \Pic(C \times_k T) \, / \, \pi_T^* \Pic(T),
18	\]
19	is, by definition, abelian-group-valued. The abelian-group structure on each
20	fiber \(\Pic(C \times_k T)\) comes from tensor product of line bundles
21	(Stacks tag 01CR / Hartshorne~II.\S 6), and the structure descends to the
22	quotient because \(\pi_T^*\) is a group homomorphism.
23	
24	% SOURCE: [Kleiman], "The Picard scheme", \S 2 (FGA Explained Ch.~9 \S 9.2),
25	% Definitions df:aPf + df:Pfs (the absolute and relative Picard functors are
26	% abelian-group-valued; the relative one is defined as a quotient of abelian
27	% groups)
28	% (read from references/kleiman-picard-src/kleiman-picard.tex, L1274-L1318).
29	% SOURCE QUOTE:
30	% "\begin{dfn}\label{df:aPf}
31	%  The {\it absolute Picard functor} $\Pic_X$ is the functor from  the
32	%  category of (locally Noetherian) $S$-schemes $T$ to the category of
33	% abelian groups defined by the formula
34	% 	$$\Pic_X(T):=\Pic(X_T).$$
35	% \end{dfn}
36	%  [\dots]
37	% \begin{dfn}\label{df:Pfs}
38	%   The {\it relative Picard functor} $\Pic_{X/S}$ is defined by
39	% 	$$\Pic_{X/S}(T):=\Pic(X_T)\big/\Pic(T).$$
40	%  Denote its associated sheaves in the Zariski, \'etale, and fppf
41	% topologies  by
42	% 	$$\Pic_{(X/S)\zar},\ \Pic_{(X/S)\et},\
43	% 	\Pic_{(X/S)\fppf}.$$
44	%  \end{dfn}"
45	\textit{Source: [Kleiman], ``The Picard scheme'', \S 2, Defs.~df:aPf + df:Pfs.
46	The absolute and relative Picard functors take values in the category of
47	abelian groups; the relative functor is the quotient of one such group by
48	another, and the structure morphisms of the resulting presheaf are group
49	homomorphisms.}
50	
51	Concretely, the abelian-group law on \(\Pic(C \times_k T)\) is the assignment
52	\(\bigl[\,\mathcal{L}\,\bigr] + \bigl[\,\mathcal{L}'\,\bigr] :=
53	\bigl[\,\mathcal{L} \otimes_{\mathcal{O}_{C \times_k T}} \mathcal{L}'\,\bigr]\),
54	with neutral element the class of the structure sheaf
55	\(\mathcal{O}_{C \times_k T}\) and inverse
56	\(- \bigl[\,\mathcal{L}\,\bigr] := \bigl[\,\mathcal{L}^{-1}\,\bigr]\), where
57	\(\mathcal{L}^{-1} = \mathcal{H}om_{\mathcal{O}_{C \times_k T}}(\mathcal{L},
58	\mathcal{O}_{C \times_k T})\) is the dual invertible sheaf. Pullback of
59	\(\mathcal{O}\)-modules along \(\pi_T : C \times_k T \to T\) preserves tensor
60	products and the structure sheaf, so the image
61	\(\pi_T^* \Pic(T) \subset \Pic(C \times_k T)\) is a subgroup, and the quotient
62	\(\Pic(C \times_k T) / \pi_T^* \Pic(T)\) inherits a canonical abelian-group
63	structure. This is the content of
64	\cref{lem:rel_pic_sharp_groupoid} in chapter \cref{chap:Picard_RelPicFunctor}.
65	
66	The mathematics is straightforward; the obstacle to formalisation is purely
67	infrastructural, and --- crucially --- much smaller than a full
68	monoidal-category construction. The group law lives on \emph{isomorphism
69	classes} of line bundles: each group axiom is a \emph{proposition} asserting
70	the existence of an isomorphism (\(\mathrm{Nonempty}(\,\cdots \cong \cdots)\)),
71	and a proposition is insensitive to which isomorphism witnesses it. In
72	particular the coherence data of a monoidal category --- the pentagon,
73	triangle and hexagon identities, and a fortiori any closed structure --- is
74	\emph{never consumed} by a group law on iso-classes. What the law genuinely
75	requires on the carrier is therefore only:
76	\begin{enumerate}
77	  \item a binary tensor-product operation
78	    \(\otimes\, : \Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X \times \Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X
79	                  \to \Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\), well-defined on
80	    isomorphism classes;
81	  \item the structure sheaf \(\mathcal{O}_X\) as a designated unit object
82	    for \(\otimes\);
83	  \item the \(\otimes\)-invertibility predicate
84	    (\(\exists N,\ M \otimes_X N \cong \mathcal{O}_X\)) together with the three
85	    monoidal coherence isomorphisms of \(\otimes_X\): associativity
86	    \((M \otimes N) \otimes P \cong M \otimes (N \otimes P)\), unit
87	    \(\mathcal{O}_X \otimes M \cong M\) (and on the right), and commutativity
88	    \(M \otimes N \cong N \otimes M\). The inverse is carried by the
89	    predicate, not by a separate lemma.
90	\end{enumerate}
91	At Mathlib's pinned commit one might be tempted to obtain (3) by instantiating a
92	\emph{full} symmetric monoidal category on \(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\) and
93	reading the three isomorphisms off it. Two Mathlib facts make such a route
94	conceivable. First, the category \(\mathtt{PresheafOfModules}\,R\) of presheaves
95	of modules over a presheaf of commutative rings \(R\) is already a (symmetric)
96	monoidal category in Mathlib (\(\mathtt{PresheafOfModules.monoidalCategory}\)),
97	with the sectionwise relative tensor \((M \otimes_R N)(U) = M(U) \otimes_{R(U)}
98	N(U)\); this applies verbatim to the \emph{varying} structure sheaf
99	\(R = \mathcal{O}_X\). Second, Mathlib's localization-of-monoidal-categories API
100	(\(\mathtt{CategoryTheory.Localization.Monoidal.LocalizedMonoidal}\)) takes a
101	monoidal category \(C\), a localization functor \(L : C \to D\) at a morphism
102	property \(W\), and an instance \(W.\mathtt{IsMonoidal}\), and produces a
103	\emph{full} monoidal-category structure on the localization \(D\); sheafification
104	of presheaves of modules is the localization \(L = \mathtt{sheafification}\) at the
105	locally-bijective class \(W = J.W\), so a full monoidal structure on
106	\(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\) would follow once \(J.W.\mathtt{IsMonoidal}\) were
107	supplied.
108	
109	This full-monoidal-category route is, however, \emph{not} the goal, and is not
110	pursued. The goal is the commutative \emph{group} on locally-trivial iso-classes
111	(à la \(\mathtt{CommRing.Pic}\)), and that group law consumes only propositions, so
112	it never needs a coherent monoidal category --- neither the
113	pentagon/triangle/hexagon identities nor the \(J.W.\mathtt{IsMonoidal}\) instance
114	that the \(\mathtt{LocalizedMonoidal}\) API requires. What it needs is exactly the
115	\emph{existence} of the three coherence isomorphisms of (3), and these are produced
116	directly: the associator by gluing canonical local isomorphisms
117	(\cref{lem:tensorobj_assoc_iso}, via the single linchpin
118	\cref{lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso}), the unitors by the cheap \(\mathtt{mapIso}\)
119	pattern (\cref{lem:tensorobj_unit_iso}), and the braiding likewise
120	(\cref{lem:tensorobj_comm_iso}). With those three existence facts the group law
121	assembles \emph{by hand} the commutative group of \(\otimes\)-invertible
122	\(\otimes\)-iso-classes --- the Picard group
123	(\cref{lem:tensorobj_isoclass_commgroup}) --- each group-axiom field fed a single
124	existence-of-isomorphism. This is the scheme-theoretic analogue of Mathlib's
125	fixed-ring \(\mathtt{CommRing.Pic}\,R := \mathrm{Skeleton}(\mathtt{Module.Invertible}\,R)\),
126	whose \(\mathtt{CommGroup}\) is read off the \(\mathrm{Skeleton}\) of the coherent
127	monoidal category \(\mathtt{MonoidalCategory}(\mathtt{Module.Invertible}\,R)\) that
128	Mathlib supplies over a fixed ring; the present construction cannot take that
129	\(\mathrm{Skeleton}\) route, since the coherent monoidal category on
130	\(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\) for the varying structure sheaf is exactly what it
131	avoids. The
132	\(\otimes\)-invertibility predicate (\cref{def:scheme_modules_isinvertible},
133	Mathlib's \(\mathtt{Module.Invertible}\) idiom) carries each inverse existentially.
134	The \emph{full} \(\mathtt{LocalizedMonoidal}\) / \(J.W.\mathtt{IsMonoidal}\) coherent
135	monoidal category is therefore not built for the group law and is off the critical
136	path; what the group law does still need from this circle of ideas is the
137	\emph{existence} of the associator (\cref{lem:tensorobj_assoc_iso}), realized as a
138	sheafification transport whose single open left-whisker obligation is closed by the
139	stalk--tensor commutation (d.2). The stalk apparatus (d.1/d.2) is accordingly
140	\emph{on} the critical path, while the coherent monoidal category itself is not.
141	
142	\section{Mathlib API survey}
143	\label{sec:tensorobj_api_survey}
144	
145	The relevant pieces of the Mathlib API at the pinned commit
146	(\texttt{b80f227}) were originally assembled into a single \emph{realization route}
147	(route~(e)): obtaining a full monoidal structure on the substrate by instantiating
148	Mathlib's abstract localization-of-monoidal-categories API rather than
149	hand-assembling the associator. That route is \emph{superseded}. The group law on
150	iso-classes never consumes a coherent monoidal category, and the associator is

1	\chapter{Abelian-variety rigidity: morphisms from a genus-\(0\) curve into an abelian variety are constant}
2	\label{chap:AbelianVarietyRigidity}
3	\label{chap:avr_for_rr}
4	% archon:covers AlgebraicJacobian/AbelianVarietyRigidity.lean AlgebraicJacobian/Genus0BaseObjects.lean AlgebraicJacobian/Genus0BaseObjects/BareScheme.lean AlgebraicJacobian/Genus0BaseObjects/ChartIso.lean AlgebraicJacobian/Genus0BaseObjects/Points.lean AlgebraicJacobian/Genus0BaseObjects/GmScaling.lean AlgebraicJacobian/RigidityLemma.lean
5	% Local macro: diagrammatic (Lean `≫`) composition. Defined locally because this
6	% chapter is the writer's only editable file; flagged in the writer report for promotion
7	% to macros/common.tex if desired. \providecommand is a no-op if it is later added globally.
8	\providecommand{\fatsemi}{\mathbin{\,;\,}}
9	% STRATEGY NOTE (iter-157). This chapter is the dedicated home of the project's COMMITTED
10	% genus-0 route (route (c)): the characteristic-free abelian-variety rigidity stack. It is
11	% 1:1 with the new upstream Lean file AlgebraicJacobian/AbelianVarietyRigidity.lean, which
12	% imports AlgebraicJacobian.Genus and is imported by AlgebraicJacobian.Jacobian (breaking the
13	% RigidityKbar -> Rigidity -> Jacobian import cycle so that genusZeroWitness can consume the
14	% keystone directly). The headline prop:rigidity_genus0_curve_to_AV is the char-free
15	% replacement (no [CharZero]) for the over-k-bar declaration rigidity_over_kbar of
16	% \cref{chap:RigidityKbar} (which remains in tree as the fallback route (a) artifact and
17	% still carries [CharZero]). The df=0 / H^0(P^1,Omega)=0 / Serre-duality framing is the
18	% fallback (a), NOT the route developed here.
19	The genus-\(0\) arm of the Jacobian-witness construction has a \emph{trivial} witness object
20	(\(J = \Spec k\)); its only remaining mathematical content is a rigidity \emph{statement}:
21	every pointed morphism from a smooth proper geometrically irreducible curve of genus \(0\)
22	into an abelian variety is the constant morphism at the identity. We prove this
23	\textbf{characteristic-free} --- the protected goal is over an arbitrary field \(k\), so the
24	cheaper differential argument (via $H^0(\mathbb P^1, \Omega) = H^0(\mathbb P^1, \mathcal
25	O(-2)) = 0\(, hence \)df = 0\(, hence \)f$ constant) is unavailable: it breaks on a
26	characteristic-\(p\) Frobenius-descent wall, where a nonconstant \(f\) can have \(df = 0\). The
27	rigidity route below sidesteps that wall.
28	The chain has two parts. The \emph{Rigidity Lemma} (\cref{thm:rigidity_lemma}, Mumford,
29	Form~I; equivalently Milne's Rigidity Theorem~1.1) is an elementary properness/closed-map
30	argument --- it uses neither the theorem of the cube nor any cohomology, is the entry point
31	for formalisation, and is now proven axiom-clean. From the Rigidity Lemma, Milne's \S I.3
32	derives the genus-\(0\) base case \emph{without} the theorem of the cube --- and, in this
33	project's reading, \emph{without} Milne's Theorem~3.2 (rational-map extension), Lemma~3.3,
34	Auslander--Buchsbaum, or differentials (the ``4th route''; see
35	\cref{rmk:base_case_fourth_route}). The decisive observation is that \(\mathbb P^1\) is
36	\emph{already complete} and \(f \colon \mathbb P^1 \to A\) is \emph{already a total morphism}, so
37	there is no rational defect map to extend over a complete surface and Theorem~3.2 never enters.
38	Instead the base case is closed by the \textbf{\(\mathbb G_m\)-scaling shortcut} (the project's
39	primary route, an Archon-original realisation of Milne Proposition~3.9): one uses the
40	\emph{multiplicative scaling} action $\sigma_\times \colon \mathbb P^1 \times \mathbb G_m \to
41	\mathbb P^1\(, \)(x, \lambda) \mapsto \lambda x\( (the M\"obius scaling fixing both \)0$ and
42	\(\infty\)), which is a \emph{genuine total} scheme morphism on all of $\mathbb P^1 \times \mathbb
43	G_m\( (\cref{def:gaTranslationP1}). Normalising \)f(0) = \eta_A\( and composing \)h := \sigma_\times
44	\fatsemi f\(, one applies additivity of \)\mathrm{Hom}(-, A)$ over a product
45	(\cref{lem:hom_additivity_over_product}, Milne Corollary~1.5) --- whose Lean signature requires
46	only the \emph{first} factor complete --- with \(V = \mathbb P^1\) proper, \(W = \mathbb G_m\), and
47	base points \(0 \in \mathbb P^1\), \(1 \in \mathbb G_m\). Because \(0\) is a \emph{fixed point} of
48	scaling, the \(W\)-axis restriction \(\lambda \mapsto h(0, \lambda) = f(\lambda \cdot 0) = f(0)\) is
49	\emph{constant} at \(\eta_A\), so the decomposition collapses to \(h = p \fatsemi f\), i.e.\
50	\(f(\lambda x) = f(x)\). Specialising \(x = 1\) gives that \(f|_{\mathbb G_m}\) is constant, and since
51	\(\mathbb G_m\) is dense in \(\mathbb P^1\) with \(A\) separated, \(f\) is constant
52	(\cref{prop:morphism_P1_to_AV_constant}, via the separated-target/dominant-source handle
53	\cref{thm:GrpObj_eq_of_eqOnOpen}). This route uses \emph{only} the proven Corollary~1.5, a
54	scaling fixed point, and density --- it never forms the additive defect map, never invokes
55	\(\mathrm{Hom}(\mathbb G_a, A) = 0\), and so sidesteps the ``image of a group homomorphism is a
56	closed subgroup'' input that has no Mathlib support. The classical \(\mathbb G_a\)-additive
57	argument (\cref{lem:hom_from_Ga_trivial} via the \(\mathbb G_a/\mathbb G_m\) incompatibility
58	\cref{lem:hom_Ga_to_av_trivial}, the heart of Milne Proposition~3.9) is retained only as the
59	Milne-faithful alternative and is \emph{off} the genus-\(0\) critical path. The corollary that a
60	pointed regular map of abelian
61	varieties is a homomorphism (\cref{lem:av_regular_map_is_hom}, Milne Corollary~1.2) is recorded
62	for completeness and for Route~A but is \emph{not} needed on the genus-\(0\) path; Milne's
63	Theorem~3.2 (\cref{lem:rational_map_to_av_extends}) is likewise demoted to a Route-A-only item
64	(the Albanese universal property), no longer on the genus-\(0\) critical path
65	(\cref{rmk:thm32_codim1_mathlib_gap}). Combining these
66	gives that every morphism \(\mathbb P^1 \to A\) is constant
67	(\cref{prop:morphism_P1_to_AV_constant}); composing with the classification of genus-\(0\)
68	curves (\cref{thm:genus_zero_curve_iso_p1}, \(C \cong \mathbb P^1\) over \(\bar k\)) yields the
69	headline \cref{prop:rigidity_genus0_curve_to_AV}. The theorem of the cube (Milne \S I.5) is
70	\emph{not} used anywhere on this path (see \cref{rmk:cube_not_needed}); the chain invokes
71	neither Serre duality nor the FGA \(\Pic\) representability engine, so the genus-\(0\) arm is
72	fully decoupled from Route~A.
73	\medskip
74	Throughout, \(\bar k\) is an algebraically closed field of arbitrary characteristic. An
75	\emph{abelian variety} over \(\bar k\) is a complete (proper) irreducible group variety; in the
76	project's Lean encoding \(A\) is an object of \(\mathrm{Over}\,(\Spec \bar k)\) carrying
77	\(\mathtt{GrpObj}\), \(\mathtt{IsProper}\), \(\mathtt{Smooth}\), and \(\mathtt{GeometricallyIrreducible}\).
78	\section{The Rigidity Lemma}
79	\begin{lemma}
80	\leanok
81	[Rigidity Lemma (Mumford, Form I)]
82	  \label{thm:rigidity_lemma}
83	  \uses{lem:rigidity_eqOn_dense_open}
84	  
85	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.rigidity_lemma}
86	  % SOURCE: [Mumford, Abelian Varieties], Rigidity Lemma (Form I.), Ch.~II \S4, book p.~43 (read from references/mumford-abelian-varieties.pdf, PDF page 54)
87	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "RIGIDITY LEMMA. (Form I.) Let \(X\) be a complete variety, \(Y\) and \(Z\)
88	  % any varieties, and \(f \colon X \times Y \to Z\) a morphism such that for some
89	  % \(y_0 \in Y\), \(f(X \times \{y_0\})\) is a single point \(z_0\) of \(Z\). Then there is a
90	  % morphism \(g \colon Y \to Z\) such that if \(p_2 \colon X \times Y \to Y\) is the
91	  % projection, \(f = g \circ p_2\)."
92	  \textit{Source: Mumford, Abelian Varieties, Ch.~II \S4, Rigidity Lemma (Form~I), p.~43.}
93	  Let \(X\) be a complete variety, and let \(Y\), \(Z\) be any varieties over \(\bar k\). Let
94	  \(f \colon X \times Y \to Z\) be a morphism such that for some \(y_0 \in Y\) the image
95	  \(f(X \times \{y_0\})\) is a single point \(z_0 \in Z\). Then there is a morphism
96	  \(g \colon Y \to Z\) with \(f = g \circ p_2\), where \(p_2 \colon X \times Y \to Y\) is the
97	  projection; that is, \(f\) depends only on the \(Y\)-coordinate, and \(g(y) = f(x_0, y)\) for any
98	  fixed \(x_0 \in X\).
99	\end{lemma}
100	% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: "PROOF. Choose any point \(x_0 \in X\), and define \(g \colon Y \to Z\) by
101	% \(g(y) = f(x_0, y)\). Since \(X \times Y\) is a variety, to show that \(f = g \circ p_2\), it is
102	% sufficient to show that these morphisms coincide on some open subset of \(X \times Y\). Let
103	% \(U\) be an affine open neighbourhood of \(z_0\) in \(Z\), \(F = Z - U\), and \(G = p_2(f^{-1}(F))\);
104	% then \(G\) is closed in \(Y\) since \(X\) is complete and hence \(p_2\) is a closed map. Further
105	% \(y_0 \notin G\) since \(f(X \times \{y_0\}) = \{z_0\}\). Therefore \(Y - G = V\) is a non-empty
106	% open subset of \(Y\). For each \(y \in V\), the complete variety \(X \times \{y\}\) gets mapped by
107	% \(f\) into the affine variety \(U\), and hence to a single point of \(U\). But this means that for
108	% any \(x \in X\), \(y \in V\), \(f(x, y) = f(x_0, y) = g \circ p_2(x, y)\), and this proves our
109	% assertion."
110	\begin{proof}
111	\leanok
112	% NOTE (iter-159 review): the iter-157 unsoundness REMAINS REPAIRED. The collapse hypothesis
113	% (_hf : f(X x {y0}) = {z0}) is threaded through all three helpers (rigidity_lemma ->
114	% rigidity_core -> rigidity_eqOn_dense_open) and GENUINELY CONSUMED in the body of
115	% rigidity_eqOn_dense_open (proves y0 \notin G, the load-bearing non-emptiness of V := Y - G).
116	% The counterexample f = fst is excluded (under f = fst, _hf forces X to collapse to a point).
117	% rigidity_eqOn_dense_open is sorry-free in its OWN body (hfib, the pullback-fibre fact over the
118	% k-bar-point y0, is closed). rigidity_snd_lift and snd_left_isClosedMap are axiom-clean (no sorryAx).
119	% NOTE (iter-160): the \uses edges run FORWARD along the true Lean dependency order
120	% (rigidity_lemma -> rigidity_eqOn_dense_open -> rigidity_eqOn_saturated_open_to_affine), so the
121	% not-proven status of the residual sorry propagates up and de-launders BOTH thm:rigidity_lemma and
122	% lem:rigidity_eqOn_dense_open. No backward edge from the leaf lemmas back up the chain.
123	% NOTE (iter-161): bridge 2 (rigidity_eqOn_saturated_open_to_affine) was DECOMPOSED into two named
124	% top-level sub-lemmas along the route-B (cohomology-free) split:
125	%   - Step 2, morphism_eq_of_eqAt_closedPoints: "two morphisms agreeing at every closed point of a
126	%     reduced Jacobson source into a separated target are equal" -- PROVEN axiom-clean (the
127	%     dense-closed-points coproduct probe + ext_of_isDominant; the connective Mathlib lacks).
128	%   - Step 1, rigidity_eqAt_closedPoint_of_proper_into_affine: Mumford's per-closed-slice constancy
129	%     -- the genuinely-deep residual, still `sorry`. THIS is now the chain's lone deep residual
130	%     (bridge 2's body is real assembly: Step 2 proven, the JacobsonSpace U instance now derivable).
131	% SIGNATURE CHANGE (iter-161, plan-authorized): Step 2's globalisation needs the closed points of
132	% the saturated open U to be DENSE, i.e. U Jacobson, which over Spec k-bar follows from (X tensor Y)
133	% being LOCALLY OF FINITE TYPE. So [LocallyOfFiniteType (X tensor Y).hom] is now carried on FIVE
134	% chain lemmas (rigidity_lemma, rigidity_core, rigidity_eqOn_dense_open,
135	% rigidity_eqOn_saturated_open_to_affine, rigidity_eqAt_closedPoint_of_proper_into_affine), in
136	% addition to [IsAlgClosed kbar]. Free downstream: curves and abelian varieties are of finite type
137	% over k-bar. The earlier "[IsAlgClosed] is the only added instance" claim is hereby retired.
138	  Fix any point \(x_0 \in X\) and define \(g \colon Y \to Z\) by \(g(y) = f(x_0, y)\) (the
139	  composite of \(y \mapsto (x_0, y)\) with \(f\)). Since \(X \times Y\) is a variety (in
140	  particular reduced and irreducible, so separated and with no embedded structure), to prove
141	  the equality of morphisms \(f = g \circ p_2\) it suffices to prove they agree on a non-empty
142	  open subset of \(X \times Y\).
143	  Let \(U\) be an affine open neighbourhood of \(z_0\) in \(Z\), put \(F = Z - U\) (a closed set),
144	  and set \(G = p_2(f^{-1}(F))\). Because \(X\) is complete, the projection
145	  \(p_2 \colon X \times Y \to Y\) is a \emph{closed} map (this is the defining property of
146	  completeness/properness); hence \(G\), the image of the closed set \(f^{-1}(F)\), is closed in
147	  \(Y\). The hypothesis \(f(X \times \{y_0\}) = \{z_0\} \subseteq U\) gives
148	  \(f^{-1}(F) \cap (X \times \{y_0\}) = \varnothing\), so \(y_0 \notin G\). Therefore
149	  \(V := Y - G\) is a non-empty open subset of \(Y\).
150	  For each \(y \in V\) we have \(X \times \{y\} \cap f^{-1}(F) = \varnothing\), i.e.
151	  \(f(X \times \{y\}) \subseteq U\). Thus the complete (proper) variety \(X \times \{y\}\) is
152	  mapped by \(f\) into the \emph{affine} variety \(U\). A proper connected variety mapping to an
153	  affine variety has image a single point (a global regular function on a complete connected
154	  variety is constant). Hence \(f(X \times \{y\})\) is one point, necessarily
155	  \(f(x_0, y) = g(y)\). So for all \(x \in X\) and \(y \in V\),
156	  \[ f(x, y) = f(x_0, y) = g(y) = (g \circ p_2)(x, y). \]
157	  The two morphisms \(f\) and \(g \circ p_2\) thus agree on the non-empty open
158	  \(X \times V\), which is dense in the irreducible \(X \times Y\); as \(Z\) is separated they
159	  agree everywhere. \qedhere
160	\end{proof}
161	\begin{remark}
162	  \label{rmk:rigidity_lemma_cube_free}
163	  The proof of \cref{thm:rigidity_lemma} uses \emph{only} (i) that completeness of \(X\) makes
164	  \(p_2\) a closed map, and (ii) that a proper connected variety has no nonconstant map to an
165	  affine variety. It uses neither the theorem of the cube nor any cohomology, and is valid in
166	  arbitrary characteristic. This is the designated formalisation entry point: it is the
167	  lowest-prerequisite link of the chain. (Mumford gives this as the geometric heart of the
168	  ``second proof'' of commutativity of an abelian variety; Milne packages the same statement
169	  as the Rigidity Theorem~1.1, requiring \(V\) complete and \(V \times W\) geometrically
170	  irreducible.)
171	\end{remark}
172	\begin{remark}
173	  \label{rmk:rigidity_lemma_decomposition}
174	  The Lean formalisation of \cref{thm:rigidity_lemma} splits into three helper steps, mirroring
175	  the proof above: \texttt{rigidity\_snd\_lift} (the cartesian-monoidal algebra identifying the
176	  collapsed map \((x, y) \mapsto (x_0, y)\) followed by \(f\) with \(g \circ p_2\));
177	  \texttt{rigidity\_core} (the scheme-level gluing: two maps out of the geometrically irreducible
178	  reduced \(X \times Y\) into the separated \(Z\) that agree on a non-empty open subset agree
179	  everywhere, by separatedness); and \texttt{rigidity\_eqOn\_dense\_open}
180	  (\cref{lem:rigidity_eqOn_dense_open}, the genuine geometry: the construction of the non-empty
181	  open \(X \times V\) and the slice-constancy of \(f\) on it). The collapse hypothesis
182	  \(f(X \times \{y_0\}) = \{z_0\}\) must be threaded through \emph{all three} helpers, since it is
183	  what makes \(V\) non-empty; a decomposition that drops it from the lower helpers is unsound
184	  (see \cref{lem:rigidity_eqOn_dense_open}).
185	  \emph{Status (iter-161).} Bridge~1 (the closed-map step) is \textbf{built} as the axiom-clean
186	  helper \texttt{snd\_left\_isClosedMap}. The fibre-over-\(y_0\) fact is \textbf{closed} (via the
187	  coarse \texttt{PullbackCarrier} layer), so \texttt{rigidity\_eqOn\_dense\_open} is
188	  \texttt{sorry}-free in its own body. Bridge~2 (slice-constancy on the saturated open \(U\),
189	  \cref{lem:rigidity_eqOn_saturated_open_to_affine}) has now been \textbf{decomposed} along its
190	  cohomology-free route-(c) split into two named sub-lemmas: the globalisation
191	  \cref{lem:morphism_eq_of_eqAt_closedPoints} (Step~2, ``agree at every closed point of a reduced
192	  Jacobson source \(\Rightarrow\) equal'') is \textbf{proven axiom-clean}, and the per-closed-slice
193	  constancy \cref{lem:rigidity_eqAt_closedPoint_of_proper_into_affine} (Step~1, Mumford's slice
194	  argument) is the chain's \emph{single genuinely-deep residual} \texttt{sorry}. The body of
195	  \cref{lem:rigidity_eqOn_saturated_open_to_affine} is therefore real assembly (Step~2 over Step~1).
196	  % NOTE: (iter-162 review) STALE as of iter-162 — Step~1 is now PROVEN and the WHOLE
197	  % Rigidity-Lemma chain (thm:rigidity_lemma … lem:rigidity_eqAt_closedPoint_of_proper_into_affine,
198	  % via the new helper lem:isIntegral_of_retract_of_integral) is sorry-free + axiom-clean
199	  % ({propext, Classical.choice, Quot.sound}, re-verified). The "single genuinely-deep residual
200	  % sorry" wording here and in the proof blocks of lem:rigidity_eqOn_dense_open / …saturated… /
201	  % …eqAt_closedPoint… should be refreshed by the plan/blueprint-writer to "chain closed iter-162".
202	  This route requires the base field to be algebraically closed \emph{and} the source to be of finite
203	  type: Step~2 needs the closed points of \(U\) to be dense (the Jacobson-space property), which over
204	  the algebraically closed \(\bar k\) holds because \(X \times Y\) is locally of finite type.
